content:
# Exodus Chapter 10 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~37r-38r (PDF pp. 71-72); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 50-51 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_010_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_010_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Eighth Plague: Locusts -- Arbeh (vv. 1-20)

**10:1** And YHWH said to Moses, "Go in to Pharaoh, for I have made heavy [kaved] his heart and the heart of his servants, so that I may set these signs of mine in his midst, [fn1]

**10:2** and so that you may recount in the ears of your son and the son of your son what I have wrought upon Egypt, and my signs which I have set among them -- and you shall know that I am YHWH." [fn2]

**10:3** And Moses and Aaron went in to Pharaoh and said to him, "Thus says YHWH, the God of the Hebrews: 'How long do you refuse to humble yourself before me? Send out my people, that they may serve me.

**10:4** For if you refuse to send out my people, behold, I am bringing tomorrow the locust into your territory.

**10:5** And it shall cover the eye of the land, and no one shall be able to see the land. And it shall eat the remnant of what has escaped -- that which remains to you from the hail -- and it shall eat every tree that sprouts for you from the field. [fn3]

**10:6** And they shall fill your houses, and the houses of all your servants, and the houses of all Egypt -- which your fathers and your fathers' fathers have not seen, from the day they were upon the ground until this day.'" And he turned and went out from Pharaoh. [fn4]

**10:7** And the servants of Pharaoh said to him, "How long shall this one be a snare to us? Send out the men, and let them serve YHWH their God. Do you not yet know that Egypt is destroyed?" [fn5]

**10:8** And Moses and Aaron were brought back to Pharaoh, and he said to them, "Go, serve YHWH your God. Who and who are the ones going?" [fn6]

**10:9** And Moses said, "With our young and with our old we will go; with our sons and with our daughters, with our flocks and with our herds we will go, for it is a feast of YHWH for us."

**10:10** And he said to them, "May YHWH be so with you -- as I would send you out with your little ones! See, for evil is before your faces. [fn7]

**10:11** Not so! Go now, the men, and serve YHWH, for that is what you are seeking." And he drove them out from the presence of Pharaoh. [fn8]

**10:12** And YHWH said to Moses, "Stretch out your hand over the land of Egypt for the locust, and it shall come up over the land of Egypt and eat every plant of the land -- all that the hail left remaining."

**10:13** And Moses stretched out his staff over the land of Egypt, and YHWH drove an east wind upon the land all that day and all that night. Morning came, and the east wind carried the locust. [fn9]

**10:14** And the locust came up over all the land of Egypt and rested upon all the territory of Egypt -- very heavy [kaved me'od]. Before it there had not been such a locust, and after it there shall not be so. [fn10]

**10:15** And it covered the eye of all the land, and the land was darkened. And it ate every plant of the land and every fruit of the tree that the hail had left remaining. And nothing green was left remaining in the tree or in the plant of the field in all the land of Egypt. [fn11]

**10:16** And Pharaoh hastened to call for Moses and for Aaron and said, "I have sinned against YHWH your God and against you.

**10:17** And now, bear, I pray, my sin only this time, and entreat YHWH your God, and let him remove from upon me only this death." [fn12]

**10:18** And he went out from Pharaoh and entreated YHWH.

**10:19** And YHWH turned a west wind, very strong, and it carried the locust and thrust it toward the Sea of Reeds. Not one locust remained in all the territory of Egypt. [fn13]

> **10:20** **And YHWH strengthened [chazaq, PIEL] the heart of Pharaoh, and he did not send out the sons of Israel.** [fn14]

---

## The Ninth Plague: Darkness -- Choshekh (vv. 21-29)

**10:21** And YHWH said to Moses, "Stretch out your hand toward the heavens, and let there be darkness over the land of Egypt -- a darkness that may be felt." [fn15]

**10:22** And Moses stretched out his hand toward the heavens, and there was a thick darkness in all the land of Egypt three days. [fn16]

**10:23** They did not see, each man his brother, and they did not rise, each man from his place, three days. But for all the sons of Israel there was light in their dwellings. [fn17]

**10:24** And Pharaoh called to Moses and said, "Go, serve YHWH. Only your flocks and your herds shall be held back. Also your little ones shall go with you." [fn18]

**10:25** And Moses said, "You yourself must also give into our hands sacrifices and burnt offerings, that we may offer them to YHWH our God. [fn19]

**10:26** And also our livestock shall go with us; not a hoof shall remain behind, for from them we shall take to serve YHWH our God, and we -- we do not know with what we shall serve YHWH until we come there." [fn20]

> **10:27** **And YHWH strengthened [chazaq, PIEL] the heart of Pharaoh, and he was not willing to send them out.** [fn21]

**10:28** And Pharaoh said to him, "Go from me! Guard yourself -- do not again see my face, for on the day you see my face you shall die." [fn22]

**10:29** And Moses said, "You have spoken rightly; I shall not again see your face." [fn23]

---

> **[fn1] v.1 -- THE FIRST DIVINE *KAVED*-HARDENING: "I have made heavy his heart"** -- Hebrew: *ki-ani hikhbadti et-libbo ve-et-lev avadav lema'an shiti ototai elleh beqirbo* (כִּי־אֲנִ֞י הִכְבַּ֤דְתִּי אֶת־לִבּוֹ֙ וְאֶת־לֵ֣ב עֲבָדָ֔יו לְמַ֗עַן שִׁתִ֛י אֹתֹתַ֥י אֵ֖לֶּה בְּקִרְבּ֑וֹ). **HARDENING VERB: kaved (כבד), Hiphil הִכְבַּדְתִּי -- YHWH is the AGENT.** This is the single most significant hardening verse since 9:12. For the first time, YHWH claims the *kaved* root -- previously used only of Pharaoh's self-hardening (8:15, 8:32, 9:34) -- as his own action: "I have made heavy." YHWH now commands both hardening verbs: *chazaq* (since 9:12) and *kaved* (here). The extension to "the heart of his servants" is new -- the hardening has become institutional, spreading from one man to his entire court. The purpose clause (*lema'an shiti ototai*) reveals that hardening serves the display of signs: YHWH hardens so that plagues may continue, so that signs may accumulate for the generational testimony of v. 2. EMET: "I have made heavy his heart and the heart of his servants"; LOGOS: "I have made heavy his heart and the heart of his servants"; PNEUMA: "I have made his heart heavy, and the hearts of his officials." All three agree on the core *kaved* rendering. The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS word order, preserving the Hebrew sequence with "heart of his servants" (not PNEUMA's "officials" for *avadav*, which is consistently rendered "servants" in the SOPHIA convention). LXX (Vaticanus): *ego gar esklēruna autou tēn kardian kai tōn therapontōn autou* ("for I have hardened his heart and his servants'") -- *sklērunō* for *kaved* hiphil, the same verb used for *chazaq* piel, collapsing the Hebrew distinction.

> **[fn2] v.2 -- *Hit'alalti*: "what I have wrought upon Egypt"** -- Hebrew: *et asher hit'alalti beMitsrayim* (אֵ֣ת אֲשֶׁ֤ר הִתְעַלַּ֨לְתִּי֙ בְּמִצְרַ֔יִם). The hitpael of *'alal* (עלל) -- "to deal severely with, to make sport of, to wrought upon." The verb carries both judgment and mockery: YHWH has not merely punished Egypt but exposed the impotence of its gods and the fragility of its power. EMET: "what I have wrought [made sport]"; LOGOS: "what I have dealt upon"; PNEUMA: "how I made fools of." The consensus renders "what I have wrought upon" -- following EMET's primary rendering. LOGOS's "dealt upon" is slightly awkward. PNEUMA's "made fools of" captures the mockery dimension but loses the gravity of divine action. "Wrought upon" preserves both the severity and the purposefulness of YHWH's actions while being suitably elevated in register. The verb *tesapper* ("recount, narrate") is rendered "recount" following LOGOS rather than EMET's "tell" or PNEUMA's "tell" -- *sapper* (piel of *saphar*) means "to recount, to narrate in detail," which is stronger than simple "telling." LXX: *hosa enepaiza tois Aiguptiois* ("how I mocked the Egyptians") -- *empaizō* ("to mock") for *hit'alalti*.

> **[fn3] v.5 -- *Ein ha-arets*: "the eye of the land"** -- Hebrew: *vekhissah et-ein ha-arets* (וְכִסָּ֣ה אֶת־עֵ֣ין הָאָ֗רֶץ). Literally "the eye of the land" -- the visible surface, the face of the ground. EMET and LOGOS both render literally: "the eye of the land." PNEUMA renders "the face of the ground," which interprets *ein* ("eye") as "face" -- a reasonable gloss but one that loses the striking Hebrew metaphor. The eye of the land is covered; the land is blinded. This image anticipates the darkness plague (vv. 21-23) where sight itself will be abolished. The consensus preserves the literal "eye of the land." The phrase *yeter hapeletah* ("the remnant of what has escaped") links the locust plague to the hail: what survived the hail is now consumed. LOGOS's "the remnant of what has escaped" captures the layered Hebrew vocabulary better than PNEUMA's simplified "whatever escaped the hail." LXX: *tēn opsin tēs gēs* ("the appearance/face of the earth") -- interpreting *ein* as "appearance."

> **[fn4] v.6 -- "And he turned and went out from Pharaoh"** -- Hebrew: *vayyifen vayyetse me'im Par'oh* (וַיִּ֥פֶן וַיֵּצֵ֖א מֵעִ֥ם פַּרְעֹֽה). Moses delivers the ultimatum and leaves without waiting for a response -- a dramatic assertion of prophetic authority. The verb *panah* ("to turn") followed by *yatsa* ("to go out") conveys abrupt departure. This walkout triggers the extraordinary scene in v. 7, where Pharaoh's own servants break ranks. All three translators capture the departure; PNEUMA's "walked out of Pharaoh's presence" is natural but loses the Hebrew verb-pair structure. The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS: "he turned and went out from Pharaoh."

> **[fn5] v.7 -- *Avdah Mitsrayim*: "Egypt is destroyed"** -- Hebrew: *haterem teda ki avdah Mitsrayim* (הֲטֶ֣רֶם תֵּדַ֔ע כִּ֥י אָבְדָ֖ה מִצְרָ֑יִם). The servants' question is the most dramatic moment in the plague negotiations. The verb *avdah* (qal perfect of *abad*, "to perish, be lost") is a completed action: Egypt *is* destroyed, not "is being destroyed" or "will be destroyed." EMET: "Mitsrayim is lost"; LOGOS: "Egypt is destroyed"; PNEUMA: "Egypt is ruined." The consensus renders "Egypt is destroyed" -- following LOGOS. "Destroyed" captures the finality of the qal perfect better than EMET's "lost" (which can sound merely misplaced) or PNEUMA's "ruined" (which suggests damage but not destruction). The word *moqesh* ("snare") identifies Moses as a source of entrapment -- though the irony is that Pharaoh's own hardened heart is the real trap. LXX: *ouk oidas hoti apolōlen Aiguptos* ("do you not know that Egypt has perished").

> **[fn6] v.8 -- *Mi vami*: "Who and who are the ones going?"** -- Hebrew: *mi vami haholkhim* (מִ֥י וָמִ֖י הַהֹלְכִ֑ים). The doubled interrogative is emphatic and demanding. EMET: "Who and who [are] the ones going?"; LOGOS: "Who exactly are the ones going?"; PNEUMA: "But exactly who will be going?" The consensus renders "Who and who are the ones going?" -- following EMET. The Hebrew doubled *mi vami* is distinctive and should not be smoothed into "exactly who" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). The doubling conveys Pharaoh's impatient, suspicious demand for precise specification. LXX: *tines kai tines hoi poreuomenoi* ("who and who are the ones going?"), preserving the doubling.

> **[fn7] v.10 -- Pharaoh's sarcasm: *yehi khen YHWH immakhem*** -- Hebrew: *yehi khen YHWH immakhem ka'asher ashalach etkhem ve-et-tappekhem re'u ki ra'ah neged peneikhem* (יְהִ֨י כֵ֤ן יְהוָה֙ עִמָּכֶ֔ם כַּאֲשֶׁ֛ר אֲשַׁלַּ֥ח אֶתְכֶ֖ם וְאֶת־טַפְּכֶ֑ם רְא֕וּ כִּ֥י רָעָ֖ה נֶ֥גֶד פְּנֵיכֶֽם). This is a bitterly sarcastic oath: "May YHWH be so with you" is an ironic blessing formula that Pharaoh immediately negates -- he will never send the children. The phrase *re'u ki ra'ah neged peneikhem* ("see, for evil is before your faces") is ambiguous: *ra'ah* may mean (a) "evil intent" -- Pharaoh accuses Moses of plotting escape; or (b) "calamity" -- Pharaoh warns of disaster. EMET: "See, for evil [is] before your faces"; LOGOS: "See, for evil is before your faces"; PNEUMA: "Clearly you have some evil scheme in mind." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS: "See, for evil is before your faces" -- preserving the ambiguity of *ra'ah*. PNEUMA resolves the ambiguity toward "evil scheme," which is one valid reading but forecloses the other. The dash after "May YHWH be so with you" marks the sarcastic pivot: the blessing is conditional on something Pharaoh will never permit. LXX: *idete hoti ponēria prokeitai humin* ("see that evil intent lies before you").

> **[fn8] v.11 -- *Gevarim*: "the men" and the expulsion** -- Hebrew: *lo khen lekhu-na hagevarim ve'ivdu et-YHWH... vayegaresh otam me'et penei Par'oh* (לֹ֣א כֵ֗ן לְכ֤וּ נָא֙ הַגְּבָרִ֔ים וְעִבְד֖וּ אֶת־יְהוָ֑ה... וַיְגָ֣רֶשׁ אֹתָ֔ם מֵאֵ֖ת פְּנֵ֥י פַרְעֹֽה). Pharaoh's counter-offer: only adult men may go. The verb *vayegaresh* (piel of *garash*, "to drive out") is the same verb used for the exodus itself (12:39). Pharaoh's micro-expulsion from the court prefigures the macro-expulsion from Egypt. EMET: "And he drove them out from the face of Par'oh"; LOGOS: "And he drove them out from the presence of Pharaoh"; PNEUMA: "And they were driven out of Pharaoh's presence." The consensus follows LOGOS: "he drove them out from the presence of Pharaoh" -- preserving the active subject (Pharaoh drives them out) while rendering *me'et penei* as "from the presence of" (more natural than EMET's "from the face of" in this context).

> **[fn9] v.13 -- *Ruach qadim*: "east wind" (MT vs. LXX south wind)** -- Hebrew: *vaYHWH nihag ruach qadim ba-arets* (וַיהוָ֗ה נִהַ֤ג ר֨וּחַ קָדִים֙ בָּאָ֔רֶץ). The MT reads *qadim* ("east") -- the hot sirocco from the desert. The LXX (Vaticanus) reads *anemon noton* ("south wind") -- a deliberate geographical adaptation reflecting Egyptian experience, where locust swarms arrive from the south (Sudan/Ethiopia) rather than the east. This is one of the clearest instances of the LXX translators adjusting the text to Egyptian geography. The verb *nihag* ("to drive, to lead") presents YHWH as the driver of the wind -- the same verb used for driving flocks. The consensus follows the MT: "east wind." All three translators agree. LXX divergence noted but not adopted.

> **[fn10] v.14 -- *Kaved me'od*: "very heavy" (the locust swarm)** -- Hebrew: *kaved me'od* (כָּבֵ֣ד מְאֹ֔ד). The adjective *kaved* describes the locust swarm -- the same root used for heart-hardening (v. 1) and for the wind that will remove the locusts (v. 19). The *kaved* saturation of this chapter is extraordinary: YHWH makes Pharaoh's heart heavy (v. 1, hiphil), the locust plague is heavy (v. 14, adjective), and the rescue wind is heavy/strong (v. 19, adjective). EMET: "very heavy"; LOGOS: "very heavy"; PNEUMA: "in devastating numbers." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS: "very heavy" -- preserving the *kaved* wordplay. PNEUMA's "devastating numbers" loses the connection entirely. The uniqueness formula ("before it... after it") parallels the hail (9:18, 24) and is echoed in Joel 2:2. LXX: *polla sphodra* ("very many") -- losing the *kaved* wordplay.

> **[fn11] v.15 -- *Vattekhshakh ha-arets*: "the land was darkened"** -- Hebrew: *vattekhshakh ha-arets* (וַתֶּחְשַׁ֖ךְ הָאָ֑רֶץ). The verb *chashakh* ("to be dark, to grow dark") creates a narrative bridge from the locust plague to the darkness plague. The locusts produce a proto-darkness that foreshadows cosmic darkness. EMET: "the land was darkened"; LOGOS: "the land was darkened"; PNEUMA: "the ground was black with them." PNEUMA's rendering interprets the darkening as caused by the locusts' physical coverage (black insect bodies), which is one reading, but the Hebrew verb *chashakh* is the standard term for darkness -- the same root as *choshekh* in vv. 21-22. The consensus preserves the verbal link: "the land was darkened."

> **[fn12] v.17 -- *Ha-mavet ha-zeh*: "this death"** -- Hebrew: *veyaser me'alai raq et-hammavet hazzeh* (וְיָסֵ֥ר מֵעָלַ֖י רַ֖ק אֶת־הַמָּ֥וֶת הַזֶּֽה). Pharaoh calls the locust plague "this death" -- not "the plague" or "the locusts" but *death itself*. The locusts consume the future: without grain, Egypt dies. EMET: "this death"; LOGOS: "this death"; PNEUMA: "this death." All three agree. The verb *sa na* ("bear, I pray") is the standard forgiveness term -- to "carry away" sin. EMET: "bear, please, my sin"; LOGOS: "bear, I pray, my sin"; PNEUMA: "please forgive my sin." The consensus renders "bear, I pray, my sin" -- following LOGOS. The Hebrew *sa* ("bear, lift") is preserved rather than PNEUMA's interpretive "forgive," which imports a later theological category. The act of "bearing" sin is the concrete image underlying forgiveness.

> **[fn13] v.19 -- *Yam Suf*: "Sea of Reeds"** -- Hebrew: *vayitqa'ehu yammah Suf* (וַיִּתְקָעֵ֖הוּ יָ֣מָּה ס֑וּף). The *Yam Suf* ("Sea of Reeds") appears here for the first time in the plague narrative, foreshadowing the crossing in chapters 13-15. The same body of water that swallows the locusts will swallow Pharaoh's army. The wind that removes the plague (*ruach-yam*, "west wind," literally "sea wind") foreshadows the wind that will part the sea. EMET: "Yam Suf [Sea of Reeds]"; LOGOS: "Sea of Reeds"; PNEUMA: "Sea of Reeds." All three agree on "Sea of Reeds" rather than the traditional "Red Sea" (which follows the LXX's *eruthran thalassan*). The consensus renders "Sea of Reeds" per SOPHIA convention. The verb *vayitqa'ehu* ("thrust it") from *taqa'* ("to drive, to fasten") is forceful: the wind drives the locusts into the sea. EMET: "thrust it toward the Yam Suf"; LOGOS: "thrust it toward the Sea of Reeds"; PNEUMA: "hurled them into the Sea of Reeds." The consensus follows LOGOS: "thrust it toward the Sea of Reeds" -- preserving the Hebrew singular ("it" for the collective locust) and the verb *taqa'* ("thrust") rather than PNEUMA's "hurled."

> **[fn14] v.20 -- YHWH strengthens Pharaoh's heart (*chazaq* piel)** -- Hebrew: *vayekhazzeq YHWH et-lev Par'oh velo shilakh et-benei Yisra'el* (וַיְחַזֵּ֥ק יְהוָ֖ה אֶת־לֵ֣ב פַּרְעֹ֑ה וְלֹ֥א שִׁלַּ֖ח אֶת־בְּנֵ֥י יִשְׂרָאֵֽל). **HARDENING VERB: chazaq (חזק), Piel וַיְחַזֵּק -- YHWH is the AGENT.** This continues the pattern of 9:12. EMET: "YHWH strengthened the heart of Par'oh"; LOGOS: "the LORD strengthened the heart of Pharaoh"; PNEUMA: "YHWH strengthened Pharaoh's heart." All three agree on the *chazaq* = "strengthened" rendering (PNEUMA now aligned with the convention). The consensus follows the SOPHIA pattern: "YHWH strengthened the heart of Pharaoh." The phrase "sons of Israel" (*benei Yisra'el*) is preserved per convention. Rendered in bold and blockquoted to mark the divine hardening.

> **[fn15] v.21 -- *Veyamesh choshekh*: "a darkness that may be felt"** -- Hebrew: *vihi choshekh al-erets Mitsrayim veyamesh choshekh* (וִיהִ֥י חֹ֨שֶׁךְ֙ עַל־אֶ֣רֶץ מִצְרָ֔יִם וְיָמֵ֖שׁ חֹֽשֶׁךְ). This is one of the most debated phrases in Exodus. The verb *yamesh* most likely derives from *mashash* (משׁש, "to feel, to grope") -- the darkness is so thick it can be physically felt, touched. EMET: "and one may feel [the] darkness"; LOGOS: "a darkness to be felt"; PNEUMA: "a darkness so thick it can be touched." The consensus renders "a darkness that may be felt" -- a formulation that draws on the traditional English rendering while preserving the Hebrew's ambiguity about who does the feeling. LOGOS's "a darkness to be felt" is close; EMET's "one may feel" is more literal but syntactically awkward; PNEUMA's "so thick it can be touched" interprets *yamesh* and adds "thick" (which is reserved for *afelah* in v. 22). The phrase "a darkness that may be felt" is retained as the consensus because: (1) it preserves the tactile sense of *mashash*; (2) it does not prematurely use "thick" (saved for *afelah*); (3) it has deep roots in English translation tradition while remaining faithful to the Hebrew. LXX: *psēlaphēton skotos* ("palpable/gropeable darkness") -- confirming the tactile reading.

> **[fn16] v.22 -- *Choshekh-afelah*: "thick darkness"** -- Hebrew: *vayehi choshekh-afelah bekhol-erets Mitsrayim sheloshet yamim* (וַיְהִ֧י חֹֽשֶׁךְ־אֲפֵלָ֛ה בְּכָל־אֶ֥רֶץ מִצְרָ֖יִם שְׁלֹ֥שֶׁת יָמִֽים). The compound *choshekh-afelah* layers two words for darkness: *choshekh* (the common term, cf. Genesis 1:2) and *afelah* (from *'apal*, "to be dark, thick, gloomy") -- dense, impenetrable murk. The combination appears in Day of YHWH texts (Joel 2:2; Zephaniah 1:15). EMET: "afelah-choshekh [thick darkness]"; LOGOS: "thick darkness"; PNEUMA: "a smothering darkness." The consensus renders "a thick darkness" -- following LOGOS. "Thick darkness" captures the compound's intensification: not merely absence of light but positive, oppressive, paralyzing gloom. EMET's transliterated form is unnecessary when the English captures the Hebrew adequately. PNEUMA's "smothering" is evocative but adds a metaphor not in the text. The three days of darkness reverse creation (Genesis 1:2-4) and parallel the three-day wilderness journey proposed in 3:18, 5:3, and 8:27. LXX: *skotos gnophos thuella* ("darkness, murk, storm") -- expanding *afelah* into two Greek terms.

> **[fn17] v.23 -- The Israel-Egypt distinction in the darkness** -- Hebrew: *lo ra'u ish et-akhiv velo qamu ish mittakhtav sheloshet yamim ulkhol-benei Yisra'el hayah or bemoshvotam* (לֹא־רָא֞וּ אִ֣ישׁ אֶת־אָחִ֗יו וְלֹא־קָ֛מוּ אִ֥ישׁ מִתַּחְתָּ֖יו שְׁלֹ֣שֶׁת יָמִ֑ים וּלְכָל־בְּנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֔ל הָ֥יָה א֖וֹר בְּמוֹשְׁבֹתָֽם). The paralysis is total: no one can see another; no one can rise from his place. The phrase *mittakhtav* ("from beneath him / from his place") suggests complete immobilization. The contrast with Israel is absolute: darkness for Egypt, light (*or*) in Israel's dwellings (*moshvotam*). This continues the distinction pattern of 8:22, 9:4, and 9:26. EMET: "They did not see, each man his brother, and they did not rise, each man from his place"; LOGOS: "They did not see one another, and no one rose from his place"; PNEUMA: "No one could see another person, and no one could rise from where they were." The consensus follows EMET's structure for the first clause ("they did not see, each man his brother") preserving the Hebrew idiom *ish et-akhiv* ("each man his brother"), but follows LOGOS's "no one rose from his place" for the second clause for clarity. "Sons of Israel" is preserved per convention; PNEUMA's "Israelites" is corrected.

> **[fn18] v.24 -- Pharaoh's final concession: "your flocks and your herds shall be held back"** -- Hebrew: *raq tso'nkhem uveqarkhem yutsag* (רַ֤ק צֹֽאנְכֶם֙ וּבְקַרְכֶ֔ם יֻצָּ֑ג). The verb *yutsag* (hophal of *yatsag/natsag*, "to be set, stationed") means the livestock must remain behind -- effectively held hostage. Pharaoh concedes children (denied in v. 11) but now withholds animals. Each plague extracts a greater concession while Pharaoh retains diminishing leverage. EMET: "shall be set [left behind]"; LOGOS: "shall be held back"; PNEUMA: "Only leave your flocks and herds behind." The consensus renders "shall be held back" -- following LOGOS. "Held back" captures both the passive verb (*yutsag*) and the restraining force of Pharaoh's demand.

> **[fn19] v.25 -- Moses' audacious demand** -- Hebrew: *gam-attah titten beyadenu zevakhim ve'olot ve'asinu laYHWH Eloheinu* (גַּם־אַתָּ֛ה תִּתֵּ֥ן בְּיָדֵ֖נוּ זְבָחִ֣ים וְעֹל֑וֹת וְעָשִׂ֖ינוּ לַיהוָ֥ה אֱלֹהֵֽינוּ). Not only will Israel take its own livestock, but Pharaoh must contribute sacrificial animals. The oppressor must fund the liberation. EMET: "Also you shall give into our hand sacrifices and burnt offerings, and we shall make [them] to YHWH our God"; LOGOS: "You yourself must also give into our hands sacrifices and burnt offerings, that we may offer them to YHWH our God"; PNEUMA: "You yourself must also provide us with sacrifices and burnt offerings so we can offer them to YHWH our God." The consensus follows LOGOS: "You yourself must also give into our hands sacrifices and burnt offerings, that we may offer them to YHWH our God." LOGOS captures the emphatic *gam-attah* ("you yourself also") and the purpose clause naturally. PNEUMA's "provide" softens the Hebrew *titten* ("give, place in hand") -- the concrete act of giving into the hand is theologically significant.

> **[fn20] v.26 -- *Lo tisha'er parsah*: "not a hoof shall remain behind"** -- Hebrew: *lo tisha'er parsah* (לֹ֤א תִשָּׁאֵר֙ פַּרְסָ֔ה). The *parsah* ("hoof") is the smallest conceivable unit of livestock -- a vivid synecdoche for total, absolute departure. Moses' refusal is maximalist and non-negotiable. The theological rationale (*va'anakhnu lo neda mah-na'avod et-YHWH ad-bo'enu shammah*, "we do not know with what we shall serve YHWH until we come there") is a statement of open-ended obedience: worship requirements await divine specification at the destination. All three translators agree on the core rendering. The consensus follows LOGOS: "not a hoof shall remain behind."

> **[fn21] v.27 -- YHWH strengthens Pharaoh's heart (*chazaq* piel)** -- Hebrew: *vayekhazzeq YHWH et-lev Par'oh velo avah leshalkham* (וַיְחַזֵּ֥ק יְהוָ֖ה אֶת־לֵ֣ב פַּרְעֹ֑ה וְלֹ֥א אָבָ֖ה לְשַׁלְּחָֽם). **HARDENING VERB: chazaq (חזק), Piel וַיְחַזֵּק -- YHWH is the AGENT.** Third direct divine hardening (after 9:12, 10:20). The verb *avah* ("to be willing") with *lo* ("not") adds a volitional dimension: "he was not willing." The hardening comes at the moment of Pharaoh's closest approach to full capitulation -- he had conceded everything but livestock. YHWH strengthens the resistance precisely when one final concession would have released Israel. All three translators agree. Rendered in bold and blockquoted.

> **[fn22] v.28 -- Pharaoh's death threat** -- Hebrew: *lekh me'alai hishamer lekha al-tosef re'ot panai ki beyom re'otkha fanai tamut* (לֵ֤ךְ מֵֽעָלַי֙ הִשָּׁ֣מֶר לְךָ֔ אַל־תֹּ֥סֶף רְא֖וֹת פָּנָ֑י כִּ֗י בְּי֛וֹם רְאֹתְךָ֥ פָנַ֖י תָּמֽוּת). The phrase *re'ot panai* ("seeing my face") is royal court language: to see the king's face means to have audience (cf. 2 Samuel 14:24, 28, 32; Esther 1:14). Pharaoh bans Moses from the royal court on pain of death. The irony is devastating: Pharaoh, whose land sits in darkness, whose people have declared Egypt destroyed, threatens death upon the agent of the God who controls life and death. EMET: "Go from me! Guard yourself -- do not again see my face, for in the day you see my face, you shall die"; LOGOS: "Go from me! Guard yourself -- do not again see my face, for on the day you see my face you shall die"; PNEUMA: "Get away from me! Make sure you never see my face again, because the day you see my face, you will die." The consensus follows LOGOS, with "on the day" slightly preferred over EMET's "in the day" for English idiom. PNEUMA's "Get away from me" is colloquial where the Hebrew (*lekh me'alai*, "go from upon me") is formal and imperious.

> **[fn23] v.29 -- *Ken dibbarta*: "You have spoken rightly"** -- Hebrew: *ken dibbarta lo-osif od re'ot panekha* (כֵּ֣ן דִּבַּ֔רְתָּ לֹא־אֹסִ֥ף ע֖וֹד רְא֥וֹת פָּנֶֽיךָ). Moses' response is laden with dramatic irony. *Ken dibbarta* ("you have spoken rightly/correctly") affirms Pharaoh's words but with a meaning Pharaoh does not intend: Moses will indeed not return to negotiate, because the next event (the death of the firstborn) will bring Pharaoh to Moses (12:31). EMET: "You have spoken rightly -- I will not again see your face"; LOGOS: "You have spoken rightly; I shall not again see your face"; PNEUMA: "You have spoken well. I will never see your face again." The consensus follows LOGOS: "You have spoken rightly; I shall not again see your face." The adverb *ken* means "rightly, correctly" -- both EMET and LOGOS capture this. PNEUMA's "well" (*tov*) is a slight mistranslation; *ken* means "correct, so, right." LXX: *eirēkas* ("you have spoken") -- omitting the affirmative adverb.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### 1. The First Divine *Kaved*-Hardening (v. 1) -- Theological Significance

All three translators agree that *hikhbadti* (hiphil of *kaved* with YHWH as subject) should be rendered "I have made heavy." This is one of the rare cases of near-total consensus on a hardening verse. The significance is immense: YHWH now claims the *kaved* root -- the very verb Pharaoh used to harden himself in 8:15, 8:32, and 9:34. What Pharaoh did to himself, YHWH now does to Pharaoh. The extension to "the heart of his servants" marks a new development: the hardening is no longer confined to one man but has become systemic. The theological irony of v. 7 -- where these same hardened servants recognize Egypt's destruction -- shows that divine hardening does not eliminate awareness of reality but overrides the will to act upon it.

### 2. *Hit'alalti* (v. 2) -- "Wrought upon" vs. "Made Fools Of" vs. "Dealt Upon"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "what I have wrought [made sport]" | Literal: dual rendering |
| LOGOS | "what I have dealt upon" | Formal: neutral verb |
| PNEUMA | "how I made fools of" | Dynamic: mockery emphasized |

The hitpael of *'alal* carries both judgment and mockery. "Wrought upon" preserves the gravity while leaving room for both senses. PNEUMA's "made fools of" is vivid but one-dimensional, reducing divine action to mockery. LOGOS's "dealt upon" is bland. The consensus takes EMET's primary rendering -- "wrought upon" -- which is sufficiently archaic-elevated to signal that something more than ordinary "dealing" is meant.

### 3. "Eye of the Land" vs. "Face of the Ground" (v. 5)

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the eye of the land" | Literal: preserves Hebrew metaphor |
| LOGOS | "the eye of the land" | Formal: preserves Hebrew metaphor |
| PNEUMA | "the face of the ground" | Dynamic: interprets *ein* as "face" |

The consensus preserves "the eye of the land" -- a striking Hebrew metaphor that should not be normalized. The land has an "eye" (its visible surface), and the locusts cover it. PNEUMA's "face" is a reasonable interpretation (*ein* can mean "surface, appearance" as well as "eye"), but "eye" is the primary meaning and creates a more vivid image. The LXX supports interpretation (*opsin*, "appearance"), but the SOPHIA convention follows the MT's concrete imagery.

### 4. Pharaoh's Sarcasm: *Re'u ki ra'ah* (v. 10) -- Preserving Ambiguity

All three translators handle Pharaoh's sarcastic blessing differently. The critical phrase *re'u ki ra'ah neged peneikhem* is deliberately ambiguous in the Hebrew: *ra'ah* means both "evil intent" and "calamity." PNEUMA resolves the ambiguity ("evil scheme"), while EMET and LOGOS preserve it ("evil is before your faces"). The consensus preserves the ambiguity -- the reader should encounter the same interpretive challenge that the Hebrew presents.

### 5. "Darkness That May Be Felt" (v. 21) -- *Veyamesh Choshekh*

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "and one may feel [the] darkness" | Literal: active subject, brackets |
| LOGOS | "a darkness to be felt" | Formal: passive infinitive |
| PNEUMA | "a darkness so thick it can be touched" | Dynamic: adds "thick," imports "touched" |

The consensus renders "a darkness that may be felt" -- a formulation rooted in English translation tradition that balances EMET's literalness with LOGOS's elegance. PNEUMA's rendering adds "thick" (reserved for *afelah* in v. 22) and "touched" (narrower than *mashash*, which means "to feel, to grope" -- encompassing a wider range of tactile perception than mere touching). The verb *yamesh* from *mashash* is preserved in its full ambiguity: the darkness can be felt -- groped, sensed, encountered as a physical presence.

### 6. "Thick Darkness" vs. "Smothering Darkness" (v. 22) -- *Choshekh-Afelah*

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "afelah-choshekh [thick darkness]" | Literal: transliterated, reversed order |
| LOGOS | "thick darkness" | Formal: standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "a smothering darkness" | Dynamic: metaphorical |

The consensus follows LOGOS: "a thick darkness." EMET reverses the Hebrew order (*choshekh-afelah* becomes "afelah-choshekh") and adds a transliteration that is unnecessary when the English captures the sense. PNEUMA's "smothering" is evocative but imports a metaphor (darkness as an agent that smothers) not present in the Hebrew compound. "Thick darkness" conveys the intensification of *afelah* over *choshekh* without adding to the text.

### 7. Recurring Convention Disagreements

**a. Divine name:** LOGOS uses "the LORD"; EMET/PNEUMA use YHWH. SOPHIA follows YHWH per established convention. (Recurring, LOW)

**b. Pronoun capitalization for God:** LOGOS capitalizes divine pronouns (My, Mine, He, Me); EMET and PNEUMA do not. SOPHIA follows lowercase convention. (Recurring, LOW)

**c. "Sons of Israel" vs. "Israelites":** EMET: "sons of Yisra'el"; LOGOS: "sons of Israel"; PNEUMA: "Israelites" / "the Israelites." SOPHIA follows "sons of Israel" per convention where the Hebrew reads *benei Yisra'el*. (Recurring, LOW)

**d. Hebrew names:** EMET uses transliterated forms (Mosheh, Par'oh, Aharon, Mitsrayim); LOGOS and PNEUMA use standard English forms (Moses, Pharaoh, Aaron, Egypt). SOPHIA follows standard English forms per convention. (Recurring, LOW)

---

## Hardening-Verb Tracking -- Exodus 10

| Verse | Hebrew Root | Form | SOPHIA Rendering | Agent | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **10:1** | **kaved (כבד)** | **Hiphil הִכְבַּדְתִּי** | **"I have made heavy his heart"** | **YHWH (FIRST DIVINE KAVED-HARDENING)** | **YHWH claims the kaved root; extends to servants** |
| **10:20** | **chazaq (חזק)** | **Piel וַיְחַזֵּק** | **"YHWH strengthened the heart of Pharaoh"** | **YHWH** | **After locusts removed; Pharaoh did not send out sons of Israel** |
| **10:27** | **chazaq (חזק)** | **Piel וַיְחַזֵּק** | **"YHWH strengthened the heart of Pharaoh"** | **YHWH** | **After darkness; Pharaoh not willing to send them out** |

**Pattern note -- TOTAL DIVINE AGENCY:** Every hardening instance in Exodus 10 has YHWH as the agent. There is no self-hardening by Pharaoh anywhere in this chapter. The transition that began at 9:12 (first divine *chazaq* piel) and continued through 10:1 (first divine *kaved* hiphil) is now complete. YHWH owns both hardening roots. Pharaoh's continued negotiation (vv. 8-11, 24-26) and rage (v. 28) show that divine hardening does not eliminate human will or awareness -- the servants still perceive Egypt's destruction (v. 7), Pharaoh still bargains and threatens -- but it confirms and intensifies the trajectory Pharaoh himself initiated. Human agency in the hardening has ceased; divine sovereignty over Pharaoh's resistance is absolute.

### Cumulative Hardening-Verb Tracker -- Exodus 4-10

| # | Verse | Root | Form | SOPHIA Rendering | Agent |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4:21 | chazaq | Hiphil (prospective) | "strengthen" | YHWH (announced) |
| 2 | 7:3 | qashah | Hiphil (prospective) | "make hard" | YHWH (announced) |
| 3 | 7:13 | chazaq | Qal (stative) | "was strengthened" | Heart (self) |
| 4 | 7:14 | kaved | Adjective | "heavy" | Heart (state) |
| 5 | 7:22 | chazaq | Qal (stative) | "was strengthened" | Heart (self) |
| 6 | 8:15 | kaved | Hiphil (causative) | "made heavy" | Pharaoh (self) |
| 7 | 8:19 | chazaq | Qal (stative) | "was strengthened" | Heart (self) |
| 8 | 8:32 | kaved | Hiphil (causative) | "made heavy" | Pharaoh (self) |
| 9 | 9:7 | kaved | Qal (stative) | "was heavy" | Heart (state) |
| **10** | **9:12** | **chazaq** | **Piel (causative)** | **"strengthened"** | **YHWH (FIRST DIVINE HARDENING)** |
| 11 | 9:34 | kaved | Hiphil (causative) | "made heavy" | Pharaoh (self) |
| 12 | 9:35 | chazaq | Qal (stative) | "was strengthened" | Heart (self) |
| **13** | **10:1** | **kaved** | **Hiphil (causative)** | **"I have made heavy"** | **YHWH (FIRST DIVINE KAVED)** |
| **14** | **10:20** | **chazaq** | **Piel (causative)** | **"strengthened"** | **YHWH** |
| **15** | **10:27** | **chazaq** | **Piel (causative)** | **"strengthened"** | **YHWH** |

**Phase analysis across Exodus 4-10:**

| Phase | Verses | Description | Agent |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1: Announcement** | 4:21, 7:3 | YHWH predicts he will harden Pharaoh | YHWH (future) |
| **Phase 2: Self-hardening** | 7:13, 7:22, 8:15, 8:19, 8:32, 9:7 | Pharaoh's heart strengthens itself or Pharaoh makes it heavy | Pharaoh / Heart |
| **Phase 3: Turning point** | 9:12 | YHWH strengthens Pharaoh's heart for the first time (chazaq piel) | YHWH |
| **Phase 4: Mixed agency** | 9:34, 9:35 | Pharaoh hardens himself again after divine hardening; heart is strengthened | Pharaoh + Heart |
| **Phase 5: Total divine control** | 10:1, 10:20, 10:27 | Every hardening is divine; YHWH claims both roots (kaved + chazaq) | YHWH exclusively |

The trajectory is irreversible. Pharaoh's choices in Phase 2 initiated a direction; YHWH's action in Phase 3 ratified it; the brief return to self-hardening in Phase 4 showed human agency persisting alongside divine; and Phase 5 reveals the final state -- a man locked inside the consequences of his own earlier freedom, now unable to choose otherwise. The tenth plague -- the death of the firstborn -- requires a Pharaoh who will not yield until death enters his own house.

---

*Consensus completed for Exodus 10:1-29 (29 verses). All readings follow the Leningrad Codex. LXX variants noted where significant (especially the south wind divergence in v. 13 and the psēlaphēton skotos in v. 21). Two plagues rendered: locusts (vv. 1-20) and darkness (vv. 21-29). Three divine hardenings marked (vv. 1, 20, 27) -- all YHWH as agent, completing the transition to total divine sovereignty over Pharaoh's resistance. Key renderings: "eye of the land" (v. 5, literal), "a darkness that may be felt" (v. 21, traditional), "a thick darkness" (v. 22, formal), "Sea of Reeds" (v. 19, per convention). The cumulative hardening-verb tracker now records 15 instances across Exodus 4-10 (including 2 prospective announcements and 13 narrative enactments). The five-phase structure of the hardening narrative is now complete through its final stage.*

--- SOPHIA
